entry.title: I Broke My GS
entry.summary:
I broke my GS.

No injuries other than my pride. Less than a second’s worth of inattention and I dropped my bike at a gas stantion while I was out joy riding this past Sunday (July 16th). The resulting chaos was impressive for such a silly little tip-over.

I …
entry.content:
I broke my GS.

No injuries other than my pride. Less than a second’s worth of inattention and I dropped my bike at a gas stantion while I was out joy riding this past Sunday (July 16th). The resulting chaos was impressive for such a silly little tip-over.

I pulled up to a pump. This is where the less than a second of inattention comes in. I didn’t ensure my kick stand was fully engaged before I started to lean the bike over. And down it went.

Initially it seemed like a total non-event. Some minor scuffs on the plastic. I proceeded to fill up. Paid for my gas and got geared back up, jumped in the saddle and turned on the power. Motion by my left foot caught my eye. I looked down to see gasoline gushing out of my motorcycle.

I was stunned. In some sort of state of shock. With my wits mostly going off in various directions I turned off the power and wheeled the bike out of the way. Had a closer look, removed a piece of ‘protective’ bodywork and saw the damaged. The exterior portion of my fuel injector had been sheered off!

As it turned out, when the bike tipped over, it was position so that the cylinder head fell just beside the pump’s concrete island, which allowed the lip of the concrete island to impact the fuel injector assembly, which is protected only by a heavy plastic cover. Not much protector when the weight of the bike is laying on it.

I was 250kms from home.

CAA to the Rescue?

I called CAA at 6:16PM. They were busy so it was a bit of a wait before someone could take my call. After initially logging the call they had to transfer me to the “Ontario East” region so I has on hold again. Finally at 7:27 a truck was on its way to pick me up!

Just kidding. At around 7:53 the dispatched called to see if the truck had arrived. No. Ok, so they’ll try calling the driver and get back to me. Text message with an ETA update.

8:12 - Dispatch calls to see if the truck has arrived. Nope. Oh… we’re having trouble getting in touch with the driver. We’ll get back to you. Text message with an ETA update.

8:34 - Dispatch calls to see if the truck has arrived. Nien. The driver is not answering his phone. So send another bloody truck! But dispatch insists on trying once more.

45 minutes goes by with no call-back from dispatch. So I call CAA again. On hold again. Transferred to East again. On hold again. Finally speaking to another dispatcher they too try calling the original driver.

9:38 - Finally dispatch sends out a new driver with a 40 minute ETA. But Lady Luck was smiling upon my that day! The new driver is there in about 20 minutes. This kid was great. He got me squared away real quick. We stopped at a Tim’s for some coffee and then headed into Nepean where we dropped the bike off at my dealership. He even dropped me off at my hotel. So, Kudos to that guy, but a pox on CAA. Dispatch should never have waited so long to send a new driver.

So, first call at 6:19PM. Dropped the bike off at around Midnight. Around 6 hours to move a motorcycle 83kms.

At the Dealership

So the next day (Monday, July 17th), I’m at the dealership well before opening. Once the shop opened I got my bike logged in and was immediately told it would be a day or two before anyonecan even look at it. For the record, if you’re a Harley-Davidson owner and you break down while travelling the nearest H-Ddealership will put your bike at the front of the line for assessment and, if possible, repair. I really truly compleely love my GS. But BMW could learn something here.

So I catch a cab to the train station and book a lift home. While waiting I called the dealership to ask if they offered anything like priority service for travellers. I was guaranteed the bike would be looked at and I’d have an estimate the same day. But no dice.

I got a call on Tuesday with an estimate. The estimate was less than I expected, but some of the parts would need to be ordered in from Germany, with a delivery time of 7 - 10 days, and I was supposed to be leaving on a trip to Maine, USA, in ten days! One of the service people did say they would try getting the parts from BMW in the US, but it didn’t sound promising.
